packet.name: H (Yale A, Oxford A, Oxford B, Indiana A, UNC B, Chicago D)

tossups.question: A popular governor of this colony gave out his own money to locals and built tents for the homeless after a fire destroyed most of this colony’s largest city on Good Friday. In this colony, many Filipino sailors who deserted their ships, called “Manilamen,” founded the town of Saint Malo. This colony, whose first Spanish governor was Alejandro “Bloody” O’Reilly, was settled in the 1780s by thousands of Canary Islanders called “Isleños”(“ees-LAYN-yohs”). This was the (emphasize) largest colony run by Esteban Miró, who entered secret negotiations with James Wilkinson. This colony, which changed hands in the Third Treaty of San Ildefonso, was given its name by the Sieur de La Salle. Robert Livingston negotiated this colony’s final transfer for 15 million dollars. For 10 points, name this part of New France that Napoleon sold to the US.
tossups.answer: Louisiana [accept French Louisiana or Spanish Louisiana; accept Louisiana Purchase; prompt on New France or Nouvelle-France until “New France” is read] (The first clue refers to Esteban Miró helping out during the 1788 Great Fire of New Orleans.)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Louisiana [accept French Louisiana or Spanish Louisiana; accept Louisiana Purchase; prompt on New France or Nouvelle-France until "New France" is read] (The first clue refers to Esteban Miro helping out during the 1788 Great Fire of New Orleans.)
tossups.question_sanitized: A popular governor of this colony gave out his own money to locals and built tents for the homeless after a fire destroyed most of this colony's largest city on Good Friday. In this colony, many Filipino sailors who deserted their ships, called "Manilamen," founded the town of Saint Malo. This colony, whose first Spanish governor was Alejandro "Bloody" O'Reilly, was settled in the 1780s by thousands of Canary Islanders called "Islenos"("ees-LAYN-yohs"). This was the (emphasize) largest colony run by Esteban Miro, who entered secret negotiations with James Wilkinson. This colony, which changed hands in the Third Treaty of San Ildefonso, was given its name by the Sieur de La Salle. Robert Livingston negotiated this colony's final transfer for 15 million dollars. For 10 points, name this part of New France that Napoleon sold to the US.

tossups.question: In the southern part of this administrative region, the development of the Lacq (“lock”) gas field brought industry near the city of Pau (“pow”). A countess of Montijo (“mon-TEE-hoh”) popularized this region’s town of Biarritz (“beer-ITS”) as a resort. This region’s capital names a wine region divided into “left-bank” and “right-bank” areas, the former of which includes Médoc. This region’s major estuary, which receives the flow of the Garonne (“guh-RON”) and (*) Dordogne (“dor-DOHN-yuh”) rivers, is named Gironde (“zhee-ROND”). In 2014, this region merged with Poitou-Charentes (“pwah-TOO shuh-RAHNT”) and Limousin (“lee-moo-ZAN”) to form the largest administrative region in France. It contains the western part of historic Gascony and most French speakers of Basque. Bordeaux (“bor-DOH”) is the capital of, for 10 points, what region, from whence hailed Henry II’s (“the second’s”) wife Eleanor?
tossups.answer: Aquitaine (“ACK-wuh-tayn” or “ah-kee-TEN”) [or Aquitania; accept Nouvelle-Aquitaine or Eleanor of Aquitaine; accept Guyenne; anti-prompt on Bordeaux or Gascony by asking “what administrative region is that part of?”; reject “Guyana” or “Occitania” or “Languedoc”]
tossups.answer_sanitized: Aquitaine ("ACK-wuh-tayn" or "ah-kee-TEN") [or Aquitania; accept Nouvelle-Aquitaine or Eleanor of Aquitaine; accept Guyenne; anti-prompt on Bordeaux or Gascony by asking "what administrative region is that part of?"; reject "Guyana" or "Occitania" or "Languedoc"]
tossups.question_sanitized: In the southern part of this administrative region, the development of the Lacq ("lock") gas field brought industry near the city of Pau ("pow"). A countess of Montijo ("mon-TEE-hoh") popularized this region's town of Biarritz ("beer-ITS") as a resort. This region's capital names a wine region divided into "left-bank" and "right-bank" areas, the former of which includes Medoc. This region's major estuary, which receives the flow of the Garonne ("guh-RON") and (*) Dordogne ("dor-DOHN-yuh") rivers, is named Gironde ("zhee-ROND"). In 2014, this region merged with Poitou-Charentes ("pwah-TOO shuh-RAHNT") and Limousin ("lee-moo-ZAN") to form the largest administrative region in France. It contains the western part of historic Gascony and most French speakers of Basque. Bordeaux ("bor-DOH") is the capital of, for 10 points, what region, from whence hailed Henry II's ("the second's") wife Eleanor?

packet.name: G – Duke A, Purdue B, Toronto B

tossups.question: A decision declaring that Gabriel Sylliboy could not hunt out of season in this province was overturned in the case R. v. Marshall. Anna Leonowens (“LEE-un-owens”) helped found a Local Council of Women in this province, which was also home to the Birchtown community of Black Loyalists. In 2010, this province posthumously apologized for the arrest of the “Rosa Parks” of Canada, Viola Desmond. The 1995 Peace Prize was awarded to the conferences named for this province’s village of Pugwash. A blizzard hampered relief for a 1917 disaster in this province that destroyed the Tufts Cove Mi’kmaq (“MEEG-mah”) community and the Acadia Sugar Refinery. That disaster in this province’s capital was caused by the SS Imo running into the explosive-ridden SS Mont-Blanc. For 10 points, name this province governed from Halifax.
tossups.answer_sanitized: Nova Scotia [or Nouvelle-Ecosse; accept New Scotland or Alba Nuadh]
tossups.question_sanitized: A decision declaring that Gabriel Sylliboy could not hunt out of season in this province was overturned in the case R. v. Marshall. The 1995 Peace Prize was awarded to the conferences named for this province's village of Pugwash. That disaster in this province's capital was caused by the SS Imo running into the explosive-ridden SS Mont-Blanc. For 10 points, name this province governed from Halifax.

tossups.question: The snorkeling “Fantastic Grandmothers” found sea snakes in this territory’s Baie des Citrons. This territory’s Pindai Caves held fossils of birds like Sylviornis, the pile-builder megapode, and the kagu. A September 24th “Citizenship Day” celebrates the 1853 annexation of this territory, whose huts topped with flèches faîtières (“flesh fet-YAIRS”) inspired Renzo Piano’s Jean-Marie Tjibaou (“chee-BAO”) Cultural Centre. The type site of the Lapita culture is in this nickel-exporting archipelago, whose penal colony held Communards like Louise Michel and the Kabyle (“kah-BEEL”) “Algerians of the Pacific.” This sui generis collectivity is home to intelligent qua-qua crows and the Kanak people, who boycotted its 2021 independence referendum under the Nouméa (“noo-MAY-uh”) Accord. For 10 points, what overseas French territory in Melanesia is named for a Latin term for Scotland?
tossups.answer: New Caledonia [or Nouvelle-Calédonie; accept New Caledonian crow; accept Grande Terre; accept Loyalty Islands or Îles Loyauté; accept Chesterfield Islands or Îles Chesterfield; accept Belep archipelago; accept Isle of Pines or Île des Pins or Kunyié]

packet.name: K – Georgia Tech B, Gettysburg, Iowa A, Kenyon A, Tennessee A, Western A

tossups.answer_sanitized: New Caledonia [or Nouvelle-Caledonie; accept New Caledonian crow; accept Grande Terre; accept Loyalty Islands or Iles Loyaute; accept Chesterfield Islands or Iles Chesterfield; accept Belep archipelago; accept Isle of Pines or Ile des Pins or Kunyie]
tossups.question_sanitized: The snorkeling "Fantastic Grandmothers" found sea snakes in this territory's Baie des Citrons. The type site of the Lapita culture is in this nickel-exporting archipelago, whose penal colony held Communards like Louise Michel and the Kabyle ("kah-BEEL") "Algerians of the Pacific." This sui generis collectivity is home to intelligent qua-qua crows and the Kanak people, who boycotted its 2021 independence referendum under the Noumea ("noo-MAY-uh") Accord. For 10 points, what overseas French territory in Melanesia is named for a Latin term for Scotland?

tossups.question: In this region, the door jambs of houses built on artificial mounds feature male–female pairs of figures with large noses called jovo or tale. A set of buildings in this region feature programmable glass louvers and slats made from iroko wood imported from Africa, and are divided into three “villages” each based around a “Great House.” Houses in this region feature spires called flèches faîtières (“flesh fet-YAIR”). “Cases” with jutting ribs make up this region’s (*) Jean-Marie Tjibaou Cultural Centre, one of the signature designs of Renzo Piano. In this region, conical thatched roofs characterize the architecture of the Kanak people. For 10 points, name this special collectivity of France in the Pacific Ocean whose capital is Nouméa.
tossups.question_sanitized: In this region, the door jambs of houses built on artificial mounds feature male-female pairs of figures with large noses called jovo or tale. A set of buildings in this region feature programmable glass louvers and slats made from iroko wood imported from Africa, and are divided into three "villages" each based around a "Great House." Houses in this region feature spires called fleches faitieres ("flesh fet-YAIR"). "Cases" with jutting ribs make up this region's (*) Jean-Marie Tjibaou Cultural Centre, one of the signature designs of Renzo Piano. In this region, conical thatched roofs characterize the architecture of the Kanak people. For 10 points, name this special collectivity of France in the Pacific Ocean whose capital is Noumea.
tossups.answer_sanitized: New Caledonia [or Nouvelle Caledonie; prompt on Melanesia or Oceania]

set.name: 2023 "Questions Based on Wikipedia Links Ophir Sent Me"

tossups.question: The composer Samuel Snaër, best known for his Mass for Three Voices, was from this city. In one piece by a composer from this city, the marking tranquillo almost condescendingly modifies an omnipresent series of staccato eighth notes with the instruction that “the thumbs of both hands execute a connected melody,” which is later interpolated to produce the (+) title effect in double time sixteenth notes. An introduction full of chromatic runs opens a waltz by a composer from this city featuring a melody with first phrase G, B-flat, high E-flat, long D, C, C, long B-flat. Though they spent most of their adult life in Paris, the composer of the Quasimodo Symphony, Edmonde (*) Dédé, was born in this city. One composer from this city died in Brazil after collapsing during a performance of their piece Tremolo; other virtuosic piano music by that composer include Pasquinade and The Dying Poet, and they wrote the symphony A Night in the Tropics. For 10 points, name this American city home to Louis Gottschalk.
tossups.answer: New Orleans [immediately declare a loss for any team that answers La Nouvelle-Orléans (then accept it)]
tossups.answer_sanitized: New Orleans [immediately declare a loss for any team that answers La Nouvelle-Orleans (then accept it)]
tossups.question_sanitized: The composer Samuel Snaer, best known for his Mass for Three Voices, was from this city. For 10 points, name this American city home to Louis Gottschalk.

tossups.question: A group of eight missionaries from this colony, including Isaac Jogues, were killed in the village of Ossernenon after a mission to the Wendat people. Many members of the Carignan-Salières (“KAR-in-yawn SAHL-ee-air”) Regiment remained in this colony after its posting here ended. Until its dissolution, the monopolistic Company of One Hundred Associates was formed to settle this colony. After this colony returned to royal control, its first (*) Intendant, Jean Talon, conducted its first census. The King’s Daughters were brought to this colony to increase the number of women here. Licensed traders known as voyageurs (“VWY-ah-zheur”) eclipsed the previously unregulated coureurs des bois (“KOO-ruhr day bwah”) in this colony’s fur trade. For 10 points, name this North American colony conquered by England and Spain after the Seven Years’ War.
tossups.answer: New France [or Nouvelle-France; prompt on Québec or Louisiane or Louisiana or French Canada or French North America or Acadia; do not accept or prompt on “Canada”]
tossups.answer_sanitized: New France [or Nouvelle-France; prompt on Quebec or Louisiane or Louisiana or French Canada or French North America or Acadia; do not accept or prompt on "Canada"]

For 10 points, name this North American colony conquered by England and Spain after the Seven Years' War.

tossups.question: A group of “Manichean” heretics in this city executed on the orders of Robert II were the first to be put to death since the fall of the Western Roman Empire. Tanneguy (“TAHN-gee”) du Châtel staged a bridge meeting to kill John the Fearless as retribution for the murder of a duke of this city responsible for the Bal des Ardents. French forces attempted to destroy a supply convoy headed for this city in the Battle of the (*) Herrings. A duchy centered on this city was often granted to younger brothers of French kings. A seven-month siege of this city during the Hundred Years’ War was lifted after the arrival of a peasant whom the English later burnt at the stake. Explorer Jean-Baptiste Bienville founded a North American colony as a “new” version of this city. For 10 points, name this city whose so-called “maid” is Joan of Arc.
tossups.answer_sanitized: Orleans [accept New Orleans or Nouvelle Orleans]
tossups.question_sanitized: A group of "Manichean" heretics in this city executed on the orders of Robert II were the first to be put to death since the fall of the Western Roman Empire. tossups.question: In a novel set in this city, a doctor staggers out of a woman’s house with a bloody head wound inflicted by the woman’s servant, who suspected them of having an affair. Before he moved to Japan, Lafcadio Hearn spent a decade as a chronicler of this city. After moving back to this city, the protagonist of a novel spends all her time painting in her attic and neglects all household duties, including receiving visitors on Tuesdays. After his family dies of yellow fever, Joseph (*) Frowenfeld moves from Philadelphia to this city in a novel in which his patient Palmyre stabs Agricola for coordinating the capture and lynching of the slave Bras Coupe. The Grandissimes is set in this city, whose nearby barrier island of Grand Isle is where Robert Lebrun first meets Edna Pontellier. For 10 points, the late 19th century authors George Washington Cable and Kate Chopin documented Creole life in what Southern city?
tossups.answer: New Orleans [or NOLA; or The Big Easy; or La Nouvelle-Orléans]
tossups.answer_sanitized: New Orleans [or NOLA; or The Big Easy; or La Nouvelle-Orleans]

tossups.question: An early governor of this colony imitated local tribes by fighting naked, thus showing off a large tattoo of a snake. General Alexandre O'Reilly led a successful attempt to annex this colony. The Inquisition agent Antonio de Sedella became famous for baptizing slaves in this colony after running afoul of its governor, Bernardo de Galvez. A critical nexus of trade in this colony was controlled by the Mandan, Hidatsa, and Arikara peoples. After the establishment of this colony, Antoine Crozat was given a twelve-year monopoly on trade in it. This colony was traded for land in Tuscany in the Third Treaty of San Ildefonso. The Sieur de Iberville helped establish this colony's largest city. Trade along a river in this colony was frequently conducted by mixed-race coureurs du bois. For 10 points, name this French colony sold to the US in a namesake 1803 “purchase”.
tossups.answer_sanitized: Louisiana [prompt on New France or Nouvelle-France]

tossups.question: A film from this movement opens with a close-up of a beetle struggling to crawl as a metaphor for the tenacity of its protagonist, Tomé. David Desser’s book about this movement is named after the film Eros + Massacre. Animated starlings and butterflies flood the screen in a scene from a film from this movement, whose protagonist accidentally kills the wrong person when a butterfly lands on his rifle. The Insect Woman and (*) Branded to Kill are films from this movement, one of whose founding members later made a film about a woman who cuts off her lover’s penis, which was controversial for including real sex scenes. That film is called In the Realm of the Senses. For 10 points, name this movement which included directors like Shohei Imamura and Nagisa Oshima, named by analogy with a French movement spearheaded by Truffaut and Godard.
tossups.answer: Japanese New Wave [or Nuberu bagu; prompt on Nouvelle Vague]
tossups.answer_sanitized: Japanese New Wave [or Nuberu bagu; prompt on Nouvelle Vague]

tossups.question: This architect conceived of his method as finding "singularities" in objects, as described in a book he co-wrote with a philosopher. A building by this architect in Nicosia contains balconies of various heights to suggest that the entire structure is "breathing." This architect also designed a "floating dome" with a pattern to allow sunlight to stream into the building for the Abu Dhabi branch of the Louvre. Another of this architect's buildings mimics a waterspout, was inspired by the nearby Montserrat mountain, and has 4,400 pixelated square windows. That building made by him has a kaleidoscopic façade and can be found in Barcelona. He used a metallic brise soleil for a façade whose "irises" let in various amounts of light and were inspired by the mashrabiya of Islamic architecture. For 10 points, name this Pritzker Prize-winning French architect who designed the Arab World Institute in Paris.

tossups.question: Patrick Blanc created a “green wall” made of living plants for a building designed by this architect, which was built without barriers and railings so that visitors could move freely between different exhibits of world art. A current project by this architect of the Musée du quai Branly features a web-like white dome roof designed to let light pass as if through palm fronds. This architect placed a massive rusty cube called Monolith on a Swiss lake, and designed a similar blue structure for the Copenhagen Concert Hall. This architect of the Abu Dhabi branch of the (*) Louvre included a cantilevered structure leading to the Mississippi called “the Endless Bridge” as part of his dark-glass exterior design for a building in Minneapolis. This man’s best-known design was inspired by nearby Montserrat and was meant to evoke a geyser. That work, which has 4,500 LEDs on its exterior, was likened to a phallus by this architect. For 10 points, name this designer of the Guthrie Theater and Barcelona’s Torre Agbar.

bonuses.leadin: Answer some questions about the film career of actress Catherine Deneuve. For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: Answer some questions about the film career of actress Catherine Deneuve. For 10 points each:
parts: Deneuve is most famous for her collaborations with Jacques Demy during this cinematic movement. In a film from this movement, a Humphrey Bogart-obsessed character kills a cop after being caught with a stolen car.

Deneuve plays a woman who turns to prostitution to fulfill masochistic fantasies in this director’s film Belle de Jour. This man worked with Salvador Dali in his debut, Un Chien Andalou (“ahn SHYEN ahn-DAH-loo”).

In a film inspired by the Dogme (“DAW-muh”) 95 movement, Deneuve plays a woman who describes Hollywood musicals to a blind character played by this actress. This actress secured an Academy Award nomination for her song “I’ve Seen It All.”
parts_sanitized: Deneuve is most famous for her collaborations with Jacques Demy during this cinematic movement. In a film from this movement, a Humphrey Bogart-obsessed character kills a cop after being caught with a stolen car.

answers: French New Wave [accept nouvelle vague] (The unnamed film is Breathless.)

bonuses.leadin: The first Black slave of this colony was a young boy from Guinea named Olivier Le Jeune (“JUN”), who was sold to a resident of this colony by the Kirke brothers. For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: The first Black slave of this colony was a young boy from Guinea named Olivier Le Jeune ("JUN"), who was sold to a resident of this colony by the Kirke brothers. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this colony. Men sent to work in this colony were nicknamed “thirty-six months” after the duration of the contract they signed with The Company of One Hundred Associates, which for a time oversaw its settlement.
The economy of New France relied heavily on this commodity. Coureurs des bois ("BWAH") were young men who mainly worked in the trade of this commodity.
This city was founded by Samuel de Champlain in 1608 on the site of an abandoned Iroquois village called Stadaconé. It is now the capital of a French-speaking province of Canada.

Derrida formulated hauntology in a book named for these things "of Marx." Marx himself wrote that one of these spooky entities was "haunting Europe."

Mark Fisher, a.k.a. "k-punk," tied hauntology to this concept in "The Metaphysics of Crackle." Interviews with Greg Tate and Tricia Rose inspired Mark Dery's 1994 essay coining this term, which Frank B. Wilderson III modified with "pessimism."

Afrofuturism [accept "Black to the Future"; accept Afropessimism; accept word forms; prompt on partial answers]
answers_sanitized: hauntology [or hantologie; prompt on haunting or ghosts or phantoms or phantasms or similar answers; accept "Post-Soviet Hauntology: Cultural Memory of the Soviet Terror"]

bonuses.leadin_sanitized: It's time to go to the opera! So for 10 points each, answer the following about opera houses.
parts_sanitized: This city's opera house took Ove Arup's engineering firm 14 years to complete, including experiencing difficulties building the "shells" designed by Jorn Utzon.

This architect's design for the Guangzhou Opera House was inspired by the banks of the Pearl River. This first woman to win the Pritzker Prize also designed the Broad (rhymes with "load") Art Museum at Michigan State University.
Lyon's opera house was designed by this French architect, who it is named after. This 2008 Pritzker winner designed the bullet-shaped Torre Agbar in Barcelona and the Guthrie Theater in Minneapolis.

bonuses.leadin: An amnesiac member of this profession named Guy Roland tries to remember his pre-World War II identity in Patrick Modiano's novel Missing Person. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this profession. A member of this profession named Wallas murders Daniel Dupont and searches for one of the title objects in Alain Robbe-Grillet's novel The Erasers.

Modiano's Missing Person won this most prestigious award in French literature in 1978. The male-dominated juries that decided this award in its early years inspired the creation of the Prix Femina, whose jury consists entirely of women.

Robbe-Grillet's The Erasers is a major example of an experimental style of French novel described by this adjective, a phrase coined by Émile Henriot. Robbe-Grillet also collaborated with Alain Resnais, a director associated with a French film “wave” described by this adjective.
answers: new [or New Novel; or French New Wave; or Nouveau Roman; or La Nouvelle Vague]
answers_sanitized: detective [accept private investigator or similar answers]
new [or New Novel; or French New Wave; or Nouveau Roman; or La Nouvelle Vague]

Robbe-Grillet's The Erasers is a major example of an experimental style of French novel described by this adjective, a phrase coined by Emile Henriot. Robbe-Grillet also collaborated with Alain Resnais, a director associated with a French film "wave" described by this adjective.

bonuses.leadin: In a film from this country, two former prison cellmates meet up many years after one of them has established a Nazi-like mega corporation that sells phonograph records. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this country home to the director of the aforementioned film, Freedom for Us, and a director known for pioneering the modern drama with films like The Rules of the Game and The Grand Illusion.

This two word phrase names a French film movement developed in the early 1960s that emphasized innovative cinematic techniques and self-conscious writing. Directors associated with this movement include Jean-Luc Godard and François Truffaut.
In one of the seminal films of the French New Wave, Breathless, Michel commits this crime during a jarring, now famous, five shot sequence.
answers: France (those directors are Jean Renoir and Rene Clair)
killing a police officer [accept shooting a police officer or equivalents; prompt on murder or manslaughter by asking what type of person Marcel kills]
answers_sanitized: France (those directors are Jean Renoir and Rene Clair)
killing a police officer [accept shooting a police officer or equivalents; prompt on murder or manslaughter by asking what type of person Marcel kills]

bonuses.leadin: Chapters in Lynn Hunt’s Inventing Human Rights and Robert Darnton’s The Great Cat Massacre detail the emotional letters about this event that the author received from readers. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this plot point that caused much lamentation among the readers of a best-selling 1761 novel. It occurs shortly after its subject, who had fallen into a lake, sends a letter to Saint-Preux asking him to care for her children.

Julie, or the New Heloise, which is sometimes considered the best-selling book of the 18th century, was written by this Geneva-born philosopher who also wrote Confessions and Reveries of a Solitary Walker.

A critique of Rousseau’s unconvincing portrayal of Julie appears in the letters of this daughter of Jacques Necker, who wrote the novel Corinne and conducted a salon in Switzerland after fleeing France due to her opposition to Napoleon.
answers: the <u>death</u> of <u>Julie</u> [accept “the <u>New Héloïse</u>” or “la <u>nouvelle Héloïse</u>” in place of “Julie”; accept obvious equivalents for “death”]
Germaine de <u>Staël</u> [or Anne Louise Germaine de <u>Staël</u>-Holstein; or Madame de <u>Staël</u>]
answers_sanitized: the death of Julie [accept "the New Heloise" or "la nouvelle Heloise" in place of "Julie"; accept obvious equivalents for "death"]
Germaine de Stael [or Anne Louise Germaine de Stael-Holstein; or Madame de Stael]

bonuses.leadin: This character becomes very emotional when his future lover says the name “Klopstock.” For 10 points each:
parts: Name this character who shoots himself with Albert's pistols after he is spurned by the married woman Lotte. His “Sorrows” title an epistolary novel by Goethe.

The Sorrows of Young Werther may have been inspired by this epistolary novel by Jean-Jacques Rousseau centering on a romance between the title daughter of Baron d'Étange and her pseudonymous tutor St. Preux.
This other epistolary novel by Pierre Ambroise Laclos centers on the rival lovers Marquise de Merteuil and Vicomte de Valmont, who attempt to out-debauch another other.
answers: Julie; or, The New Heloise [or Julie, ou la nouvelle Héloïse]
answers_sanitized: Julie; or, The New Heloise [or Julie, ou la nouvelle Heloise]

bonuses.leadin: This archipelago was the destination of the most observation teams sent to the Indian Ocean to observe the 1874 Transit of Venus. For 10 points each:
answers: France [or Fifth French Republic; or Cinquième République Française; or Ve République]
parts: Name this archipelago in the Southern Indian Ocean, which lies on a namesake underwater plateau and whose largest island is La Grande Terre.
The Kerguelen Islands are owned by this country, whose other Indian Ocean possessions include Réunion and Mayotte. It also owns Guadeloupe and Martinique as well as a region east of Suriname.
This is France’s only special collectivity. This Melanesian region’s Kanak people won semi-autonomy via the 1998 Nouméa Accord.
answers_sanitized: Kerguelen Islands [or Desolation Islands; or Archipel des Kerguelen; or Iles Kerguelen]
France [or Fifth French Republic; or Cinquieme Republique Francaise; or Ve Republique]
